revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''大津神社'''(おおつじんじゃ)は、[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲にある[[神社]]である。 == 催事 == 同神社では年に2回祭り...'
revision.text:
'''大津神社'''(おおつじんじゃ)は、[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲にある[[神社]]である。
== 催事 ==
同神社では年に2回祭りが開催され、付近の道路に通行規制が敷かれる。秋は[[だんじり|地車]]が曳航されるため、規制が増す。
*夏祭り・・・毎年7月7、8日に開催される祭り。付近に祭りが少ないため、子供連れでごった返す。 *秋祭り・・・毎年10月7、8日に開催される祭り。市内の向野地区から地車が曳航される。
== 交通 == *[[近鉄南大阪線]][[高鷲駅]]南口から徒歩すぐ。祭礼開催時は、賑わっているところが同神社である。 *[[近鉄バス]]高鷲駅前下車すぐ。(土曜日の昼間1本のみ。) *羽曳野市公共施設循環バス「う～すけ」高鷲駅前下車すぐ。(こちらも本数は少ない。)

'''大津神社'''(おおつじんじゃ)は、[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲にある[[神社]]である。
== 催事 ==
同神社では年に2回祭りが開催され、付近の道路に通行規制が敷かれる。秋は[[だんじり|地車]]が曳航されるため、規制が増す。
*夏祭り・・・毎年7月7、8日に開催される祭り。付近に祭りが少ないため、子供連れでごった返す。 *秋祭り・・・毎年10月7、8日に開催される祭り。市内の向野地区から地車が曳航される。
== 交通 == *[[近鉄南大阪線]][[高鷲駅]]南口から徒歩すぐ。 *[[近鉄バス]]高鷲駅前下車すぐ。(土曜日の昼間1本のみ。) *羽曳野市公共施設循環バス「う～すけ」高鷲駅前下車すぐ。(こちらも本数は少ない。)

同神社では年に2回祭りが開催され、付近の道路に通行規制が敷かれる。秋は[[だんじり|地車]]が曳航されるため、規制が増す。
*夏祭り・・・毎年7月7、8日に開催される祭り。付近に祭りが少ないため、子供連れでごった返す。 *秋祭り・・・毎年10月7、8日に開催される祭り。市内の向野地区から地車が曳航される。
== 交通 == *[[近鉄南大阪線]][[高鷲駅]]南口から徒歩すぐ。 *[[近鉄バス]]高鷲駅前下車すぐ。(土曜日の昼間1本のみ。) *羽曳野市公共施設循環バス「う～すけ」高鷲駅前下車すぐ。(こちらも本数は少ない。) {{stub}} [[Category:羽曳野市|おおつしんしや]]

'''大津神社'''(おおつじんじゃ)は、[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲にある[[神社]]である。普段は人通りが少なく、静かである。
== 催事 ==
同神社では年に2回祭りが開催され、付近の道路に通行規制が敷かれる。秋は[[地車]]が曳航されるため、規制が増す。
*夏祭り・・・毎年7月7、8日に開催される祭り。付近に祭りが少ないため、子供連れでごった返す。 *秋祭り・・・毎年10月7、8日に開催される祭り。市内の向野地区から地車が曳航される。
== 交通 == *[[近鉄南大阪線]][[高鷲駅]]南口から徒歩すぐ。 *[[近鉄バス]]高鷲駅前下車すぐ。(土曜日の昼間1本のみ。) *羽曳野市公共施設循環バス「う～すけ」高鷲駅前下車すぐ。(こちらも本数は少ない。) {{stub}} [[Category:羽曳野市|おおつしんしや]]

'''大津神社'''(おおつじんじゃ)は、[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲にある[[神社]]で、[[延喜式神名帳]]に記載されている、[[河内国]][[丹比郡]]の[[式内社]]。
受験合格、安産のご利益があるとされる。 神社前は普段は人通りが少なく、静かである。
==概要== 渡来人・百済国王族の子孫である津氏一族が氏神として建立したとされている。
祭神は、 [[スサノオ|素盞嗚命]]、[[奇稻田姫命]]、[[天日鷲命]]。
== 催事 == 同神社では年に2回祭りが開催される。都度、付近の道路に通行規制が敷かれる。秋は[[地車]]が曳航されるため、規制が増える。
*夏祭り：毎年7月7、8日に開催される祭り。付近に祭りが少ないため、子供連れでごった返す。 *秋祭り：毎年10月7、8日に開催される祭り。市内の向野地区から地車が曳航される。
== 交通 == *[[近鉄南大阪線]][[高鷲駅]]南口から徒歩すぐ。 *[[近鉄バス]]高鷲駅前下車すぐ。（土曜日の昼間1本のみ） *羽曳野市公共施設循環バス「う～すけ」高鷲駅前下車すぐ。（こちらも本数は少ない）
==外部リンク== [http://kamnavi.jp/en/kawati/ootu.htm 大津神社]（神奈備）
{{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:大阪府の神社|おおつしんしや]] [[Category:羽曳野市|おおつしんしや]]

{{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおつしんしや}} [[Category:羽曳野市の神社]] [[Category:式内小社]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{神社| 名称=大津神社| 所在地=大阪府羽曳野市高鷲8-1-2| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|34|11.34|N|135|34|58.05|E|}}| 祭神=[[スサノオ|素盞嗚命]]、[[奇稻田姫命]]、[[天日鷲神|天日鷲命]]| 社格=式内社（小）| 創建=| 本殿=| 例祭=| 画像=[[Image:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|260px]]| }} '''大津神社'''(おおつじんじゃ)は、[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲にある[[神社]]で、[[延喜式神名帳]]に記載されている、[[河内国]][[丹比郡]]の[[式内社]]。
厄除開運、縁結び、安産、家内安全、交通安全、引越しや住居のリフォーム等の方災除けのご利益があるとされる。おついたち(一日)は一日参りで賑わうが、平素は人通りが少なく、静かである。羽曳野市内の氏神様として認識されている。
== 概要 ==
=== 祭神 === *[[スサノオ|素盞嗚命]](すさのおのみこと) *[[奇稻田姫命]](くしいなだひめのみこと) *[[天日鷲神|天日鷲命]](あめのひわしのみこと)
=== 配祀 === *大山咋命(おおやまくいのみこと) *菅原道真公(すがわらのみちざねこう)
=== 由緒 === 当大津神社は、十世紀はじめ、醍醐天皇(885～930)の御代に編纂された『延喜式』(927)に 「大津神社三座鍬・靱」と明記されている由緒の古い神社で、「丹下の郷の大宮」と称えられていた。 応仁天皇(4～5世紀はじめ)の御代に、文字をはじめ各種の大陸文化が朝鮮半島を経由して我国に伝来した際、当河内地方は難波の港と大和を結ぶ要路であったことから渡来人が土着し、荒地を開拓し産業を興して、遂に河内文化の繁栄を見ることに至った。 当時、この地方には、百済貴須王(近仇首王)の子孫といわれる「葛井氏・船氏・津氏」の三氏が勢力を張っており、この三氏のうち津氏一族がこの地をトして「大宮山」と称し、自分達の守護神を奉斎したことが「大津神社」の発祥だろうと云うのが古来からの定説である。 このように、大津神社は津氏一族の守護神として創祀されたが、津氏一族が朝廷に召されて大和に移住し、また時代の推移に伴って氏姓制度が衰退していくと、中世以降には、大津神社はこの地方九ヶ村の人々の「氏神」として受け継がれ、「河内の大宮」と称えられた。 その後、仏教の隆盛に伴い、『本地垂迹説』に基づいて「午頭天王社」と称し、境内に宮寺真言宗大宮山南之坊を設けて神仏混淆となり、社僧の支配を受けることとなる。広く世の人々からは、「北宮の午頭さん」と称えられ、親しまれた。 明治維新になると、新しい神社制度が定められ、神仏混淆が厳しく禁じられたため、高鷲・埴生両村の氏神として明治5年5月村社に列格され、「神道大津神社」となった。 第二次大戦後は、占領政策の神道指令によって国家の管理を離れ、昭和21年6月2日、宗教法人令に基づき神社本庁所属の「宗教法人 大津神社」として発足、現在に至る。 今日では、羽曳野市西部、南西部、広域の氏神(産土神、鎮守神)として崇敬されている。
== 催事 == 同神社では年に2回祭りが開催される。都度、付近の道路に通行規制が敷かれる。秋は[[地車]]が曳航されるため、規制が増える。
*夏祭り：毎年7月7、8日に開催される祭り。付近に祭りが少ないため、子供連れでごった返す。 *秋祭り：毎年10月7、8日に開催される祭り。市内の向野地区から地車が曳航される。
== 交通 == *[[近鉄南大阪線]][[高鷲駅]]南口から徒歩すぐ。 *[[近鉄バス松原営業所 | 近鉄バス]]高鷲駅前下車すぐ。（土曜日の昼間1本のみ） *羽曳野市公共施設循環バス「う～すけ」高鷲駅前下車すぐ。（こちらも本数は少ない）
{{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおつしんしや}} [[Category:羽曳野市の神社]] [[Category:式内小社]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{神社| 名称=大津神社| 所在地=大阪府羽曳野市高鷲8-1-2| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|34|11.34|N|135|34|58.05|E|}}| 祭神=[[スサノオ|素盞嗚命]]、[[奇稻田姫命]]、[[天日鷲神|天日鷲命]]| 社格=式内社（小）| 創建=| 本殿=| 例祭=| 画像=[[Image:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|260px]]| }}

=== 配祀 === *大山咋命(おおやまくいのみこと) *菅原道真公(すがわらのみちざねこう)

=== 祭神 === *[[スサノオ|素盞嗚尊]](すさのおのみこと) *[[奇稻田姫命]](くしいなだひめのみこと) *[[天日鷲神|天日鷲命]](あめのひわしのみこと)
=== 配祀 === *大山咋命(おおやまくいのみこと) *菅原道真公(すがわらのみちざねこう)
=== 由緒 === 大津神社は、十世紀はじめ、醍醐天皇(885～930)の御代に編纂された『延喜式』(927)に 「大津神社三座鍬・靱」と明記されている由緒の古い神社で、「丹下の郷の大宮」と称えられていた。 応仁天皇(4～5世紀はじめ)の御代に、文字をはじめ各種の大陸文化が朝鮮半島を経由して我国に伝来した際、当河内地方は難波の港と大和を結ぶ要路であったことから渡来人が土着し、荒地を開拓し産業を興して、遂に河内文化の繁栄を見ることに至った。 当時、この地方には、百済貴須王(近仇首王)の子孫といわれる「葛井氏・船氏・津氏」の三氏が勢力を張っており、この三氏のうち津氏一族がこの地をトして「大宮山」と称し、自分達の守護神を奉斎したことが「大津神社」の発祥だろうと云うのが古来からの定説である。 このように、大津神社は津氏一族の守護神として創祀されたが、津氏一族が朝廷に召されて大和に移住し、また時代の推移に伴って氏姓制度が衰退していくと、中世以降には、大津神社はこの地方九ヶ村の人々の「氏神」として受け継がれ、「河内の大宮」と称えられた。 その後、仏教の隆盛に伴い、『本地垂迹説』に基づいて「午頭天王社」と称し、境内に宮寺真言宗大宮山南之坊を設けて神仏混淆となり、社僧の支配を受けることとなる。広く世の人々からは、「北宮の午頭さん」と称えられ、親しまれた。 明治維新になると、新しい神社制度が定められ、神仏混淆が厳しく禁じられたため、高鷲・埴生両村の氏神として明治5年5月村社に列格され、「神道大津神社」となった。 第二次大戦後は、占領政策の神道指令によって国家の管理を離れ、昭和21年6月2日、宗教法人令に基づき神社本庁所属の「宗教法人 大津神社」として発足、現在に至る。 今日では、羽曳野市西部、南西部、広域の氏神(産土神、鎮守神)として崇敬されている。
== 催事 == 同神社では年に2回祭りが開催される。都度、付近の道路に通行規制が敷かれる。秋は[[地車]]が曳航されるため、規制が増える。
*夏祭り：毎年7月7、8日に開催される祭り。付近に祭りが少ないため、子供連れでごった返す。 *秋祭り：毎年10月7、8日に開催される祭り。市内の向野地区から地車が曳航される。
== 交通 == *[[近鉄南大阪線]][[高鷲駅]]南口から徒歩すぐ。 *[[近鉄バス松原営業所 | 近鉄バス]]高鷲駅前下車すぐ。（土曜日の昼間1本のみ） *羽曳野市公共施設循環バス「う～すけ」高鷲駅前下車すぐ。（こちらも本数は少ない）

{{神社| 名称=大津神社| 所在地=大阪府羽曳野市高鷲8-1-2| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|34|11.34|N|135|34|58.05|E|}}| 祭神=[[スサノオ|素盞嗚尊]]、[[奇稻田姫命]]、[[天日鷲神|天日鷲命]]| 社格=式内社（小）| 創建=| 本殿=| 例祭=| 画像=[[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|260px]]| }}
== 概要 == '''大津神社'''(おおつじんじゃ)は、[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲にある[[神社]]で、[[延喜式神名帳]]に記載されている、[[河内国]][[丹比郡]]の[[式内社]]。 厄除開運、縁結び、安産、家内安全、交通安全、引越しや住居のリフォーム等の方災除けのご利益があるとされる。おついたち(一日)は一日参りで賑わうが、平素は人通りが少なく、静かである。羽曳野市内の氏神様として認識されている。
=== 祭神 === *[[スサノオ|素盞嗚尊]](すさのおのみこと) *[[奇稻田姫命]](くしいなだひめのみこと) *[[天日鷲神|天日鷲命]](あめのひわしのみこと)
=== 配祀 === *大山咋命(おおやまくいのみこと) *菅原道真公(すがわらのみちざねこう)

河内国大宮（通称） |札所等= |例祭= |神事= }} '''大津神社'''(おおつじんじゃ)は、[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲にある[[神社]]。[[式内社]]で、[[旧社格]]は[[村社]]。 == 概要 == 厄除開運、縁結び、安産、家内安全、交通安全、引越しや住居のリフォーム等の方災除けのご利益があるとされる。おついたち(一日)は一日参りで賑わうが、平素は人通りが少なく、静かである。羽曳野市内の氏神様として認識されている。 == 祭神 == ; 主祀神 *[[スサノオ|素盞嗚尊]](すさのおのみこと) *[[奇稻田姫命]](くしいなだひめのみこと) *[[天日鷲神|天日鷲命]](あめのひわしのみこと) ; 配祀神 *[[大山咋命]](おおやまくいのみこと) *[[菅原道真]]公(すがわらのみちざねこう) == 歴史 == 大津神社は、十世紀はじめ、[[醍醐天皇]]の御代に編纂された[[延喜式神名帳]]の[[河内国]][[丹比郡]]に「大津神社三座鍬・靱」と明記されている由緒の古い神社で、「丹下の郷の大宮」と称えられていた。当時、この地方には、[[近仇首王]]（貴須王）の子孫といわれる葛井氏・船氏・津氏の三氏が勢力を張っており、この三氏のうち津氏一族がこの地をトして「大宮山」と称し、自分達の守護神を奉斎したことが大津神社の発祥だろうという古来からの定説がある。 大津神社は津氏一族の守護神として創祀されたが、津氏一族が朝廷に召されて大和に移住し、また時代の推移に伴って氏姓制度が衰退していくと、中世以降には、大津神社はこの地方九ヶ村の人々の氏神として受け継がれ、「河内の大宮」と称えられた。その後、仏教の隆盛に伴い、[[本地垂迹説]]に基づいて「午頭天王社」と称し、境内に[[真言宗]]の宮寺大宮山南之坊を設けて神仏混淆となり、社僧の支配を受けることとなる。広く世の人々からは「北宮の午頭さん」と称えられ、親しまれた。 明治維新になると、新しい神社制度が定められ、神仏混淆が厳しく禁じられたため、高鷲・埴生両村の氏神として1872年（明治5年）5月村社に列格され、大津神社と改称された。第二次大戦後は、占領政策の神道指令によって国家の管理を離れ、1946年（昭和21年）6月2日、宗教法人令に基づき[[神社本庁]]所属の宗教法人としての大津神社が発足し現在に至る。今日では、羽曳野市西部、南西部、広域の氏神(産土神、鎮守神)として崇敬されている。 == 年間行事 == 同神社では年に2回祭りが開催される。都度、付近の道路に通行規制が敷かれる。秋は[[地車]]が曳航されるため、規制が増える。 *夏祭り：毎年7月7、8日に開催される祭り。付近に祭りが少ないため、子供連れでごった返す。 *秋祭り：毎年10月7、8日に開催される祭り。市内の[[向野 (羽曳野市)|向野地区]]から地車が曳航される。 ==境内== 本殿、拝殿、幣殿からなる社殿は1640年（寛永17年）に建立されたものとされている。 === 境内社 === *大宮戎社（祭神：[[えびす|戎大神]]） *大宮稲荷社（祭神：[[宇迦之御魂大神]]） *辯天宮（べんてんぐう）（祭神：[[市杵嶋姫命]]） *平和之社（やわらぎのやしろ）（祭神：平和之神） - [[サンフランシスコ講和条約]]締結を記念して建立されたもので、氏子出身の戦没英霊278柱を祀っている。 == 現地情報 == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} ;所在地 *[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲8-1-2 ;交通アクセス * 最寄駅：[[近鉄南大阪線]][[高鷲駅]]下車後、南口から徒歩すぐ。 * 最寄バス停：[[近鉄バス松原営業所 | 近鉄バス]]高鷲駅前下車すぐ。（土曜日の昼間1本のみ） * 最寄バス停：羽曳野市公共施設循環バス「う～すけ」高鷲駅前下車すぐ。 == 外部リンク == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html 大津神社 - 大阪府神社庁] {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおつしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:羽曳野市]] [[Category:羽曳野市の歴史]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]]

河内国大宮（通称） |札所等= |例祭= |神事= }} '''大津神社'''(おおつじんじゃ)は、[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲にある[[神社]]。[[式内社]]で、[[旧社格]]は[[村社]]。 == 概要 == 厄除開運、縁結び、安産、家内安全、交通安全、引越しや住居のリフォーム等の方災除けのご利益があるとされる。おついたち(一日)は一日参りで賑わうが、平素は人通りが少なく、静かである。羽曳野市内の氏神様として認識されている。 == 祭神 == ; 主祀神 *[[スサノオ|素盞嗚尊]](すさのおのみこと) *[[奇稻田姫命]](くしいなだひめのみこと) *[[天日鷲神|天日鷲命]](あめのひわしのみこと) ; 配祀神 *[[大山咋命]](おおやまくいのみこと) *[[菅原道真]]公(すがわらのみちざねこう) == 歴史 == 大津神社は、十世紀はじめ、[[醍醐天皇]]の御代に編纂された[[延喜式神名帳]]の[[河内国]][[丹比郡]]に「大津神社三座鍬・靱」と明記されている由緒の古い神社で、「丹下の郷の大宮」と称えられていた。当時、この地方には、[[近仇首王]]（貴須王）の子孫といわれる葛井氏・船氏・津氏の三氏が勢力を張っており、この三氏のうち津氏一族がこの地をトして「大宮山」と称し、自分達の守護神を奉斎したことが大津神社の発祥だろうという古来からの定説がある。 大津神社は津氏一族の守護神として創祀されたが、津氏一族が朝廷に召されて大和に移住し、また時代の推移に伴って氏姓制度が衰退していくと、中世以降には、大津神社はこの地方九ヶ村の人々の氏神として受け継がれ、「河内の大宮」と称えられた。その後、仏教の隆盛に伴い、[[本地垂迹説]]に基づいて「午頭天王社」と称し、境内に[[真言宗]]の宮寺大宮山南之坊を設けて神仏混淆となり、社僧の支配を受けることとなる。広く世の人々からは「北宮の午頭さん」と称えられ、親しまれた。 明治維新になると、新しい神社制度が定められ、神仏混淆が厳しく禁じられたため、高鷲・埴生両村の氏神として1872年（明治5年）5月村社に列格され、大津神社と改称された。第二次大戦後は、占領政策の神道指令によって国家の管理を離れ、1946年（昭和21年）6月2日、宗教法人令に基づき[[神社本庁]]所属の宗教法人としての大津神社が発足し現在に至る。今日では、羽曳野市西部、南西部、広域の氏神(産土神、鎮守神)として崇敬されている。 == 年間行事 == 同神社では年に2回祭りが開催される。都度、付近の道路に通行規制が敷かれる。秋は[[地車]]が曳航されるため、規制が増える。 *夏祭り：毎年7月7、8日に開催される祭り。付近に祭りが少ないため、子供連れでごった返す。 *秋祭り：毎年10月7、8日に開催される祭り。市内の[[向野 (羽曳野市)|向野地区]]から地車が曳航される。 ==境内== 本殿、拝殿、幣殿からなる社殿は1640年（寛永17年）に建立されたものとされている。 === 境内社 === *大宮戎社（祭神：[[えびす|戎大神]]） *大宮稲荷社（祭神：[[宇迦之御魂大神]]） *辯天宮（べんてんぐう）（祭神：[[市杵嶋姫命]]） *平和之社（やわらぎのやしろ）（祭神：平和之神） - [[サンフランシスコ講和条約]]締結を記念して建立されたもので、氏子出身の戦没英霊278柱を祀っている。 == 現地情報 == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} ;所在地 *[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲8-1-2 ;交通アクセス * 最寄駅：[[近鉄南大阪線]][[高鷲駅]]下車後、南口から徒歩すぐ。 * 最寄バス停：[[近鉄バス松原営業所 | 近鉄バス]]高鷲駅前下車すぐ。（土曜日の昼間1本のみ） * 最寄バス停：羽曳野市公共施設循環バス「う～すけ」高鷲駅前下車すぐ。 == 外部リンク == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html 大津神社 - 大阪府神社庁] {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおつしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:羽曳野市の建築物]] [[Category:羽曳野市の歴史]]

河内国大宮（通称） |札所等= |例祭= |神事= }} '''大津神社'''(おおつじんじゃ)は、[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲にある[[神社]]。[[式内社]]で、[[旧社格]]は[[村社]]。 == 概要 == 厄除開運、縁結び、安産、家内安全、交通安全、引越しや住居のリフォーム等の方災除けのご利益があるとされる。おついたち(一日)は一日参りで賑わうが、平素は人通りが少なく、静かである。羽曳野市内の氏神様として認識されている。 == 祭神 == ; 主祀神 *[[スサノオ|素盞嗚尊]](すさのおのみこと) *[[奇稻田姫命]](くしいなだひめのみこと) *[[天日鷲神|天日鷲命]](あめのひわしのみこと) ; 配祀神 *[[大山咋命]](おおやまくいのみこと) *[[菅原道真]]公(すがわらのみちざねこう) == 歴史 == 大津神社は、十世紀はじめ、[[醍醐天皇]]の御代に編纂された[[延喜式神名帳]]の[[河内国]][[丹比郡]]に「大津神社三座鍬・靱」と明記されている由緒の古い神社で、「丹下の郷の大宮」と称えられていた。当時、この地方には、[[近仇首王]]（貴須王）の子孫といわれる[[葛井氏]]・[[船氏]]・[[津氏]]の三氏が勢力を張っており、この三氏のうち津氏一族がこの地をトして「大宮山」と称し、自分達の守護神を奉斎したことが大津神社の発祥だろうという古来からの定説がある。 大津神社は津氏一族の守護神として創祀されたが、津氏一族が朝廷に召されて大和に移住し、また時代の推移に伴って氏姓制度が衰退していくと、中世以降には、大津神社はこの地方九ヶ村の人々の氏神として受け継がれ、「河内の大宮」と称えられた。その後、仏教の隆盛に伴い、[[本地垂迹説]]に基づいて「牛頭天王社」と称し、境内に[[真言宗]]の宮寺大宮山南之坊を設けて神仏混淆となり、社僧の支配を受けることとなる。広く世の人々からは「北宮の牛頭さん」と称えられ、親しまれた。 明治維新になると、新しい神社制度が定められ、神仏混淆が厳しく禁じられたため、高鷲・埴生両村の氏神として1872年（明治5年）5月村社に列格され、大津神社と改称された。第二次大戦後は、占領政策の神道指令によって国家の管理を離れ、1946年（昭和21年）6月2日、宗教法人令に基づき[[神社本庁]]所属の宗教法人としての大津神社が発足し現在に至る。今日では、羽曳野市西部、南西部、広域の氏神(産土神、鎮守神)として崇敬されている。 == 年間行事 == 同神社では年に2回祭りが開催される。都度、付近の道路に通行規制が敷かれる。秋は[[地車]]が曳航されるため、規制が増える。 *夏祭り：毎年7月7、8日に開催される祭り。付近に祭りが少ないため、子供連れでごった返す。 　2022年から、夏祭りは屋台が出ない。 *秋祭り：毎年10月7、8日に開催される祭り。市内の[[向野 (羽曳野市)|向野地区]]から地車が曳航される。 ==境内== 本殿、拝殿、幣殿からなる社殿は1640年（寛永17年）に建立されたものとされている。 === 境内社 === *大宮戎社（祭神：[[えびす|戎大神]]） *大宮稲荷社（祭神：[[宇迦之御魂大神]]） *辯天宮（べんてんぐう）（祭神：[[市杵嶋姫命]]） *平和之社（やわらぎのやしろ）（祭神：平和之神） - [[サンフランシスコ講和条約]]締結を記念して建立されたもので、氏子出身の戦没英霊278柱を祀っている。 == 現地情報 == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} ;所在地 *[[大阪府]][[羽曳野市]]高鷲8-1-2 ;交通アクセス * 最寄駅：[[近鉄南大阪線]][[高鷲駅]]下車後、南口から徒歩すぐ。 * 最寄バス停：[[近鉄バス松原営業所 | 近鉄バス]]高鷲駅前下車すぐ。（土曜日の昼間1本のみ） * 最寄バス停：羽曳野市公共施設循環バス「う～すけ」高鷲駅前下車すぐ。 == 外部リンク == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html 大津神社 - 大阪府神社庁] {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおつしんしや}} [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:羽曳野市の建築物]] [[Category:羽曳野市の歴史]]

revision.text:
'''大津神社'''（おおつじんじゃ）は、[[神社]]。
== 主な大津神社 == *[[大津神社 (飛騨市)|大津神社]] – [[岐阜県]][[飛騨市]][[神岡町 (岐阜県)|神岡町]] *[[大津神社 (伊勢市)|大津神社]] – [[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] ： [[豊受大神宮]]末社（[[伊勢神宮|神宮]]125社のうちの1社） *[[大津神社 (泉大津市)|大津神社]] – [[大阪府]][[泉大津市]]若宮町 ： 泉大津えびす *[[大津神社 (羽曳野市)|大津神社]] – 大阪府[[羽曳野市]]高鷲 ： 旧村社 *[[大津神社 (東大阪市)|大津神社]] – 大阪府[[東大阪市]]水走 *[[大津神社 (西脇市)|大津神社]] – [[兵庫県]][[西脇市]]嶋 ： 旧郷社、津萬の厄神さん *[[大津神社 (真庭市)|大津神社]] – [[岡山県]][[真庭市]]余野下 *[[大津神社 (大分市)|大津神社]] – [[大分県]][[大分市]]口戸
{{曖昧さ回避}} {{デフォルトソート:おおつしんしや}} [[Category:同名の神社]]

'''大津神社'''（おおつじんじゃ）は、「大津」を社名に持つ[[神社]]。

revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name = Ootsu Shrine |Image = [[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|250px]]
Shrine front view |Location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko}} |Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Founded = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other Names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: - {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} - {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} - {{ill|Amewakahiko|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: - {{ill|Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} - {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyō-chō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Kazuraki no Ōkimi|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == Location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[category:new pages]] [[ja:大津神社]]

Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == Location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:01-17]][[ja:大津神社]]

{{Shrine |Name = Ootsu Shrine |Image = [[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|250px]]
Shrine front view |Location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko}} |Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Founded = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other Names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyō-chō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Kazuraki no Ōkimi|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == Location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:01-17]][[ja:大津神社]]

Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == Location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORTŌtsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

{{Shrine |Name = Ootsu Shrine |Image = [[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|250px]]
Shrine front view |Location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko}} |Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Founded = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other Names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyō-chō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Kazuraki no Ōkimi|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == Location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == Location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Kazuraki no Ōkimi|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == Location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

{{Shrine |Name = Ootsu Shrine |Image = [[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|250px]]
Shrine front view |Location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko}} |Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |Founded = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other Names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == Location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

{{Shrine |name = Ootsu Shrine |image = [[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|250px]]
Shrine front view |location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko}} |type = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |consecration_year = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

Shrine front view |location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko}} |type = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |consecration_year = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:大津神社]]

It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)}][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大津神社]]

{{Shrine |name = Ootsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|250px]]
Shrine front view |location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko}} |type = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |consecration_year = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:大津神社]]

While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:大津神社]]

Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946..
