It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:大津神社]]

{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko}} |type = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |consecration_year = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[ja:大津神社]] [[Category:New]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Ootsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|250px]]
Shrine front view |location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko}} |type = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |consecration_year = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[ja:大津神社]] [[Category:New]]

Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[ja:大津神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

{{Shrine |name = Ootsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|250px]]
Shrine front view |location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko}} |type = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}} |consecration_year = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[ja:大津神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:New]]

While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[ja:大津神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)}}

It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[ja:大津神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)}} [[Category:New]]

It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線|12=draft}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅|12=draft}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所|12=draft}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[ja:大津神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)}}

{{Shrine |name = Ootsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|250px]]
Shrine front view |location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|12=draft}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|12=draft}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=draft}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko|12=draft}} |type = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|12=draft}} |consecration_year = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|12=draft}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|12=draft}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=draft}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命|12=draft}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命|12=draft}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命|12=draft}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō|12=draft}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡|12=draft}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王|12=draft}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|12=draft}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|12=draft}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|12=draft}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|12=draft}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財|12=draft}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|12=draft}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命|12=draft}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神|12=draft}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神|12=draft}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約|12=draft}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線|12=draft}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅|12=draft}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所|12=draft}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[ja:大津神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)}}

It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=draft|12=draft}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命|12=draft|12=draft}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命|12=draft|12=draft}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命|12=draft|12=draft}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō|12=draft|12=draft}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王|12=draft|12=draft}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|12=draft|12=draft}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|12=draft|12=draft}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|12=draft|12=draft}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|12=draft|12=draft}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神|12=draft|12=draft}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神|12=draft|12=draft}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約|12=draft|12=draft}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅|12=draft|12=draft}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所|12=draft|12=draft}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[ja:大津神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)}}

'''大津神社'''（おおつじんじゃ）は、｢大津」を社名に持つ[[神社]]。
== 主な大津神社 == *[[大津神社 (飛騨市)|大津神社]] – [[岐阜県]][[飛騨市]][[神岡町 (岐阜県)|神岡町]] *[[大津神社 (伊勢市)|大津神社]] – [[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] ： [[豊受大神宮]]末社（[[伊勢神宮|神宮]]125社のうちの1社） *[[大津神社 (泉大津市)|大津神社]] – [[大阪府]][[泉大津市]]若宮町 ： 泉大津えびす *[[大津神社 (羽曳野市)|大津神社]] – 大阪府[[羽曳野市]]高鷲 ： 旧村社 *[[大津神社 (東大阪市)|大津神社]] – 大阪府[[東大阪市]]水走 *[[大津神社 (西脇市)|大津神社]] – [[兵庫県]][[西脇市]]嶋 ： 旧郷社、津萬の厄神さん *[[大津神社 (真庭市)|大津神社]] – [[岡山県]][[真庭市]]余野下 *[[大津神社 (大分市)|大津神社]] – [[大分県]][[大分市]]口戸
{{曖昧さ回避}} {{デフォルトソート:おおつしんしや}} [[Category:同名の神社]]

{{Shrine |name = Ootsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|250px]]
Shrine front view |location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |type = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |consecration_year = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|qq=draft|qq=draft}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[ja:大津神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)}} {{translated page|ja|大津神社|version=103377387|comment=Imported full ja history}}

However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|qq=draft|qq=draft}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] {{citation needed|Otsu Shrine (Habikino)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)}} {{translated page|ja|大津神社|version=103377387|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] {{translated page|ja|大津神社|version=103377387|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |type = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |consecration_year = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|qq=draft|qq=draft}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] {{translated page|ja|大津神社|version=103377387|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[Category:11]] }} [[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] {{translated page|ja|大津神社|version=103377387|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Village shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] }} [[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Former Village shrines" to "Former Village Shrines"
revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Ootsu Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka) haiden.jpg|250px]]
Shrine front view |location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Takawashi 8-chome 1-2 | Latitude = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 34 | Latitude Seconds = 11.34 | N = N | Longitude = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 34 | Longitude Seconds = 58.05 | E = E | Map Country Code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka |Deities = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |type = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |consecration_year = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|qq=draft|qq=draft}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == {{Footnote Help}} === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] {{translated page|ja|大津神社|version=103377387|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] }} [[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

{{ill|Amaterasu's Messenger|ja|天日鷲命|lt=Amewakahiko|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |type = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (small)
Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |consecration_year = Traditional: 6th century |Main Hall = |Other names = Tange no Taisha (丹下の大社) |Pilgrimage Sites = |Festivals = Autumn Grand Festival (October 7–8) |Rituals = }} '''Ootsu Shrine''' (大津神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: ** {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Kushinadahime|lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|奇稻田姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Ame-no-Hiwashi|ja|天日鷲命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * Secondary deities: ** {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|ja|大山咋命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == History == The shrine's founding is traditionally attributed to the 6th century. However, it is recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Engishiki Jinmyō-chō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} compiled during Emperor Daigo’s reign in the early 10th century as "Ootsu Shrine, three seats, Kuwari, and Yoroi" in the {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Tanpi District|ja|丹比郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It has long been known as "The Grand Shrine of Tange no Sato."[https://www.otsujinja.net/ Ootsu Shrine Official Website - History]. In ancient times, this area was dominated by three clans—{{ill|Geungusu of Baekje|ja|近仇首王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}’s descendants: the Kazuraki, Funashi, and Tsu clans. The Tsu clan established the shrine in this area, naming the mountain "Ōmiya Mountain" and enshrining their guardian deity. While originally serving as the guardian shrine for the Tsu clan, their relocation to the {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the decline of the {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|qq=draft|qq=draft}} led to the shrine becoming a regional guardian for nine villages. During the rise of Buddhism, the shrine adopted the name "Gozu Tennō Shrine" and incorporated Buddhist elements, establishing a Shingon sect temple, Nanno Bō, within its grounds.. In the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shinto-Buddhist syncretism was prohibited, and the temple was abolished. The shrine was renamed Ootsu Shrine in 1872 and designated as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.. Following the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine became a religious corporation under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1946.. Today, it is revered as the guardian deity for western and southwestern {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, offering blessings for disaster prevention, safe childbirth, and household safety. == Precincts == * Main Shrine (designated {{ill|Cultural property|ja|文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Habikino City|ja|羽曳野市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) - Rebuilt in 1640 (Kan'ei 17)[https://www.city.habikino.lg.jp/ 羽曳野市ホームページ 大津神社]. * Worship Hall - Rebuilt in 1640. * Heiden (offering hall) - Rebuilt in 1640. * Shrine Office. === Subsidiary Shrines === * Ōmiya Benten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima-hime-no-Mikoto|ja|市杵嶋姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Features an octagonal Buddhist-style hall.. * Ōmiya Ebisu Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ebisu|ja|戎大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1987.. * Ōmiya Inari Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] {{translated page|ja|大津神社|version=103377387|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] }} [[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[ja:大津神社]]

Built in 1972.. * Peace Shrine (Yawaragi-no-Yashiro) - Dedicated to the spirits of 278 war dead from the local community. Built in 1951 to commemorate the signing of the {{ill|San Francisco Peace Treaty|ja|サンフランシスコ講和条約|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/San Francisco Peace Treaty|qq=}}.. == Cultural Properties == === Habikino City Designated Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine, with plaques. == Festivals == Two festivals are held annually, during which local roads are regulated: * Summer Festival: Held annually on July 7–8. It attracts families but has no food stalls since 2022. * Autumn Festival: Held annually on October 7–8, featuring portable shrines (mikoshi) and floats. == location == {{commons|Category:Otsu-jinja (Habikino, Osaka)}} * Osaka Prefecture, Habikino City, Takawashi 8-chome 1-2. == Access == * {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|qq=}} {{ill|Takawashi Station|ja|高鷲駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takawashi Station|qq=}} - About a 3-minute walk. * Nearest bus stop: {{ill|Kintetsu Bus|ja|近鉄バス松原営業所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu Bus|qq=}} Takawashi Station, 1 trip on Saturdays. * Nearest municipal bus stop: Habikino City Circulation Bus, "Usuke" Takawashi Station. == References == === Notes === {{Notelist2}} === Sources === {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai11sibu/habikino-city/m23_02_habikino_ootu.html Ootsu Shrine - Osaka Prefecture Shrine Office] {{translated page|ja|大津神社|version=103377387|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Habikino City]] [[Category:History of Habikino City]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Ōtsu Shrine errors]] }} [[en:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[sn:Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino)]] [[ja:大津神社]]
